slides.headline: "One-stop" is the most valuable status in sync licensing.
slides.eyebrow: Education · What "one-stop" really means — and how to get there
fit_warnings: [01 hook.eyebrow] copy (63 chars) exceeds slot budget (48 at 44px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body: Most composers know the term. Almost none fully understand how to get there — especially once a label is involved.
ONE-STOP = you grant both licences from a single conversation.

LICENCE 1: The sync licence
→ Controls: use of your composition in visual media
→ Held by: the publisher — which is you, if you self-publish

LICENCE 2: The master licence
→ Controls: the specific recording
→ Held by: whoever owns the master — often a label, unless you recorded independently If you control both → one-stop.
If a label controls the master → the supervisor has to negotiate with two parties, on two timelines, with two chances for the deal to fall apart. Supervisors strongly prefer one-stop. When time is short (it always is), they go there first. IF YOUR LABEL HOLDS YOUR MASTERS:
→ Check your deal: do you have sync approval rights? Even partial co-approval?
→ Negotiate co-approval on new work before signing
→ Build one-stop status into your next deal explicitly IF YOU'RE FULLY INDEPENDENT:
→ Distribute through a platform that doesn't claim master rights (most don't — but check)
→ Self-publish: register directly with your PRO
→ Document every track's rights status from day one The path to one-stop is mostly paperwork.
It's the most commercial thing you can do for your catalogue right now. #OneClearance #SyncLicensing #AfricanComposers #SyncMaster
